pred_reason: SWC-107 is appropriate because the contract's release() function uses an external call to transfer ERC20 tokens before updating state, creating a classic reentrancy vulnerability. This allows malicious tokens to recursively call release() and drain all funds, matching the exact pattern described in SWC-107. Evidence: release() calls _token.safeTransfer(_beneficiary, amount) before state updates; Malicious ERC20 tokens with callbacks can reenter release() repeatedly; Impact confirms complete drainage of all locked tokens (100% fund loss)

findings.Issue: Reentrancy Vulnerability with Malicious ERC20 Tokens
findings.Description: The release() function is vulnerable to reentrancy attacks when interacting with malicious ERC20 tokens that implement callbacks in their transfer function. This issue appears in the smart contract code and was identified during the audit analysis.
findings.Impact: Complete drainage of all locked tokens, resulting in 100% fund loss. Malicious tokens could repeatedly call back into release() before balance updates.
findings.Location: TokenTimelock.release() function, specifically the _token.safeTransfer(_beneficiary, amount) call

findings.Issue: Missing Access Control on Release Function
findings.Description: The release() function lacks access control restrictions, allowing anyone to trigger token release once the timestamp condition is met. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential griefing attacks, front-running, and loss of beneficiary control over release timing. Could cause unexpected token transfers and gas cost issues.
findings.Location: TokenTimelock.release() function - no access modifier restricting callers

findings.Description: The constructor lacks validation to ensure the beneficiary address is not the zero address. This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Potential loss of funds if tokens are sent to the zero address, making them irrecoverable.

findings.Description: The contract relies on block.timestamp for release conditions, which can be manipulated by miners within ~900 seconds. This issue appears in both the smart contract code and static analysis results.
findings.Impact: Minor timing manipulation possible, though unlikely to significantly impact long-term timelock functionality.

findings.Description: Constructor parameters shadow function names (token, beneficiary, releaseTime), which can cause confusion but doesn't affect functionality. This issue appears in the static analysis results only.
findings.Impact: Reduced code readability and potential maintenance issues, but no functional impact.
findings.Location: TokenTimelock.constructor() parameter names shadowing function names
